steps.explanation: We only have addition and double-angle formulae, so we write $3\theta$ as the compound angle $2\theta + \theta$ to make them apply.
Using $\sin(A + B) = \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$ with $A = 2\theta$ and $B = \theta$ expands the compound angle.
Replacing $\sin 2\theta$ moves everything towards single-angle ratios.
Because the target answer is written purely in $\sin\theta$, we pick the version of $\cos 2\theta$ that contains only sine.
Putting both expansions in place gives an expression in $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ only.
Multiplying out each product separates the terms so we can deal with the remaining $\cos^2\theta$.
The Pythagorean identity converts the last cosine term into sine so the whole answer is in $\sin\theta$.
Only sines remain now; expanding the bracket will let us collect like terms.
Distributing $2\sin\theta$ across $(1 - \sin^2\theta)$ gives $2\sin\theta - 2\sin^3\theta$, leaving four terms to gather.
The sine terms give $2\sin\theta + \sin\theta = 3\sin\theta$ and the cubic terms give $-2\sin^3\theta - 2\sin^3\theta = -4\sin^3\theta$.
At $\theta = 30^\circ$ the left side is $\sin 90^\circ = 1$ and the right side also equals $1$, supporting the identity.
The LHS reduces to the RHS for all $\theta$, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Writing $\sin 3\theta = \sin(2\theta + \theta)$ and expanding with the addition and double-angle formulae, then using $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta$, gives $3\sin\theta - 4\sin^3\theta$, so $\sin 3\theta \equiv 3\sin\theta - 4\sin^3\theta$.
